posts.name: Troy Harris
posts.cooked:
Hello all! I was hoping to gain some insight on the best way to obtain some Kalin Cellars offerings. I’ve seen some stuff on winebid but that’s pretty much it. Could someone point me in the right direction on where to start?

Looking more for whites than reds.

Thanks, Troy

posts.cooked: I think @William_Kelley bought the whole cellar?

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked: Boy, I used to love their Chards back in the 80s/90s. What happened to them? When did they stop production?

posts.name: joe w i n o g r a d
posts.cooked:
If you were not aware, Terry Leighton died in 2023. William Kelley wrote a nice remembrance. What remains is in private collections and will appear at auction occasionally.

World Of Fine Wine – 9 Mar 23

Terrance James Leighton (1944–2023): High roads less traveled at Kalin Cellars

William Kelley pays tribute to Terry Leighton, the self-effacing genius behind Kalin Cellars, California’s most idiosyncratic winery.

Est. reading time: 5 minutes
posts.display_username: joe w i n o g r a d

posts.name: Chris CrutchfieId
posts.cooked:
IIRC they usually released the wines like 20+ years past vintage, correct? Surely there must have been a fair amount of unreleased wine before Terry passed?

From the obituary:

In their last declining years, the pace of Kalin releases slowed and then stopped, but admirers of California’s most idiosyncratic winery will be pleased to know that several hitherto unsold vintages are still waiting in the wings

reply_to_user.name: joe w i n o g r a d

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Kalin stopped producing after the 2001 vintage. So there is a fair bit of unreleased wine from the late 1990s through 2001, and a bit of “library” stock going back to the very first days of the winery in the mid 1970s! There are also some sparkling wines, including late disgorged stuff from 1989, 1990, 1991 and 1992 which are some of the most interesting traditional method I’ve tried from outside Champagne.

posts.name: Troy Harris
posts.cooked: How would I go by obtaining some of these?

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

There are also some sparkling wines, including late disgorged stuff from 1989, 1990, 1991 and 1992 which are some of the most interesting traditional method I’ve tried from outside Champagne.

Totally agree! Was fortunate to source a couple bottles of 1988 Sparkling Rose and the one i opened last year was magic!

1988 Kalin Cellars Cuvee Rose Reserve, California Sparkling Wine. I purchased this recently from an absolutely pristine cellar where it had been stored, untouched, since release. So after the recent praise on the Blanc from the one and only @wgfkelley , needless to say I was pretty damn excited to open this. As always, my guy Mr. Kelley was spot on. This was absolutely incredible! As in…one of the better sparkling wines I’ve had in a long time. Beautiful pale salmon color with plenty or bubbles (see the 3rd pic/video) and a great nose filled with yeasty citrus and strawberries. In the mouth this is just amazing. Vinuous, energetic, and very fresh with more strawberries, baked bread, and some slightly bitter minerality on the very long finish. Blind, you would be hard pressed to clock this as a 35 year old sparkling wine from CA. A total stunner and a contender for one of my top wines of the year. Life is good!

A question for the more experienced Kalin drinkers here…I recently bought two bottles of 1979 pinot noir, listed as cuvee DD, but as they just arrived, they’re labeled Cuvee D. I’ve only seen the single “D” used on chardonnay (and CT seems to back this up)…anyone have any info on these bottles?

@William_Kelley maybe?

IMG_51003024×4032 3.17 MB

posts.name: Andrew Northup
posts.cooked: Also a quick PSA, Verve Chicago has some bottles of 98 chard Cuvee PR at decent pricing (dropped more this morning but seems to be all that’s left)

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
I can only help with one note on one of the DDs (maybe just a mislabeling to put the one D on?). This is from 9 years ago at my cellar depressurization (109 bottles opened) event:

1984 Kalin Cellars Cuvee DD Pinot Noir

One of the more fascinating bottles, I had no idea what I’d get here. Well…even with a full day open, tons of interesting earth, light barnyard and underlying raspberry with slash of rhubarb. And this is a beauty to put in my mouth, because this is in the old style of Cali Pinot, where vivacity and acidity are king. So much active sparkly red berry fruit and amazing liveliness for, remember, 32 year old wine. A true and rare marvel, one of the great delights of my day. #4 for sheer audacity and best of the reds.

posts.name: William Kelley

This was pre-DD, and I believe came from Dutton Ranch!

DD came from David Demostene’s vineyard in Alexander Valley which is now part of Silver Oak/Twomey AFAIK.

had sourced their sauvignon blanc for many years.

@William_Kelley , do you by chance know the story of the 2001 Beau Rivage Chardonnay? Assuming that had an impact on your winery project, but wasn’t sure. Have 1 in the cellar so would love to know

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Terry and Frances made their two one-off “Beau Rivage” wines, Chenin and Chardonnay, essentially to prove that one could make something characterful and barrel fermented at a rather reasonable price. I haven’t had the Chardonnay, but the Chenin is still drinking well today! And yes, it was the inspiration for my Chenin project…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Yeah, quite what the exact vineyard origin is I’m not able to say, but it’s from Potter Valley, where they used to source quite a bit of fruit, so a really rather cool location - plus a cooler, later vintage in 1998. So it’s one of the more Chablisien-styled wines they made (but when I say Chablis think e.g. 80s-90s Dauvissat, not contemporary oyster liqor-and-lemon zest).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
My experience with the wines in vintages including the riper ones such as '79, '83, '86 and '89 and also some of the later harvest where there was a bit of botrytis such as 1993 is that in addition to all the oyster shell you frequently get a lot of honey, mandarin, orange blossom, saffron and sometimes even exotic fruit, all while remaining very racy. In the higher appellation wines, this is combined with quite some density and texture. I remember once serving a 1986 Droin Valmur to a winemaker in Beaune, whose initial gut response was “this doesn’t taste like Chablis”. Of course, it did! But not like a 2014 Chablis…

Musing on this further, Chablis is perhaps one of the regions that gets type cast the most. I think people sometimes tend to treat it as something of the Muscadet of Burgundy, and go to it looking for a very specific set of flavors, often to pair with specific cuisines (especially oysters etc). And the textbook definition of what Chablis is in many consumers’ heads is probably a young Petit Chablis from a cooler but clean year: stony, citrusy, racy, medium-bodied and bone dry. But without even getting into the global warming discussion, which tends to dominate this topic, not all vintages do that, and nor are all sites adapted to it (think Bougros, at the furthest extreme of richness, or the frequent exotica of Beauroy). And I think some of those wines that don’t fit the mold tend to get short shrift (unless of course they carry the Raveneau or Dauvissat label). This is not to say that everyone should like them, but just that they deserve more of a chance than they sometimes get.
